methods.summary: Initialise the Conv1d module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Fold BN → fake-quant folded weight → conv → (ReLU) → fake-quant output.

methods.name: from_float
methods.summary: Quantize a calibrated float convolution module.

members.extended:
A trainable float `lucid.nn.Conv1d` that fake-quantizes both the weight
and the output on every forward through a straight-through estimator (STE), so the
network adapts its weights to the eventual int8 grid while still training in full
precision.  Inserted in place of a float conv by
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`; `lucid.quantization.convert` later
bakes the trained kernel and the observed qparams into an inference
`lucid.nn.quantized.Conv1d`.

parameters.name: qconfig
parameters.description:
Quantization recipe supplying the weight and activation
`lucid.quantization.FakeQuantize` modules applied during training.

parameters.name: *args
parameters.description:
Remaining arguments are forwarded verbatim to the float
`lucid.nn.Conv1d` constructor (`in_channels`, `out_channels`,
`kernel_size`, `stride`, `padding`, `dilation`, `groups`, `bias`).

parameters.name: **kwargs
parameters.description:
Remaining arguments are forwarded verbatim to the float
`lucid.nn.Conv1d` constructor (`in_channels`, `out_channels`,
`kernel_size`, `stride`, `padding`, `dilation`, `groups`, `bias`).

notes:
The weight fake-quant rounds the kernel while the STE passes gradients straight
through, keeping the layer fully differentiable.  String padding (`"same"` /
`"valid"`) is deferred, matching the quantized conv.

parameters.name: model
parameters.description: See `fused_step` for semantics.

parameters.name: loss_fn
parameters.description: See `fused_step` for semantics.

parameters.name: optimizer
parameters.description: See `fused_step` for semantics.

parameters.name: grad_scaler
parameters.description:
See `fused_step`.  When provided and enabled, the
trace records the scale → unscale → found_inf → conditional
update plumbing entirely inside the executable so the
user-facing step is identical to a no-scaler call.

raises.description:
If `optimizer` exposes no trainable parameters (every
param_group is empty).

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Generate anchors for all FPN levels.

parameters.name: feature_maps
parameters.description: One (B, C, H, W) tensor per FPN level.

parameters.name: image_size
parameters.description:
(H, W) of the input image (unused here, kept for
          API symmetry with clip_boxes callers).

parameters.name: strides
parameters.description: (stride_h, stride_w) per level.

returns.description: List of (H_l × W_l × A_l, 4) anchor tensors, one per level.

members.name: ConvReLU1d
members.signature: ConvReLU1d(args: object = (), qconfig: QConfig | None = None, kwargs: object = {})

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Fold BN → fake-quant folded weight → conv → (ReLU) → fake-quant output.

members.extended:
Behaves like `Conv1d`, but the activation fake-quant observes the range
*after* the fused ReLU, so the calibrated output grid reflects the true
(non-negative) inference range.  Built from a fused float
`lucid.nn.intrinsic.ConvReLU1d` by
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`; `lucid.quantization.convert` folds it
into a single quantized `lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU1d`.

parameters.name: qconfig
parameters.description:
Quantization recipe supplying the weight and activation
`lucid.quantization.FakeQuantize` modules applied during training.

parameters.name: *args
parameters.description:
Remaining arguments are forwarded to the float `lucid.nn.Conv1d`
constructor, exactly as for `Conv1d`.

parameters.name: **kwargs
parameters.description:
Remaining arguments are forwarded to the float `lucid.nn.Conv1d`
constructor, exactly as for `Conv1d`.

notes:
The weight and the post-ReLU output are both fake-quantized every forward via a
straight-through estimator, so gradients reach the float kernel unchanged.

members.name: Linear
members.signature: Linear(in_features: int, out_features: int, bias: bool = True, qconfig: QConfig | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(in_features: int, out_features: int, bias: bool = True, qconfig: QConfig | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Tensor from Python data, a NumPy array, or another Tensor.
methods.extended:
The data is funnelled through `lucid._factories.converters._to_impl`,
the canonical bridge between the outside world and Lucid's C++
`TensorImpl`. Python scalars become 0-d tensors; nested lists are
recursively flattened with shape inference; NumPy arrays cross the
single sanctioned host-to-engine boundary; and existing `Tensor`
sources are rewrapped (optionally with cast and/or device transfer).

parameters.name: data
parameters.annotation: ndarray | list | int | float | bool | Tensor
parameters.description:
Source data. Python scalars become 0-d tensors; lists are
recursively converted; NumPy arrays use the bridge boundary
documented in `lucid._factories.converters`; another Tensor
is shallow-copied to a new Tensor with possibly different
`dtype` / `device` / `requires_grad`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Element type. If `None`, inferred from `data`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Target device (`"cpu"` or `"metal"`). If `None`, defaults
to the source device or `"cpu"` for host data.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Whether to record operations on this tensor for autograd.

returns.description:
A freshly constructed tensor whose storage is owned by the
engine.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> lucid.Tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
tensor([1., 2., 3.])
>>> lucid.Tensor([[1, 2], [3, 4]], dtype=lucid.int64).shape
(2, 2)
>>> x = lucid.Tensor(3.14, requires_grad=True)
>>> x.requires_grad
True
notes:
Constructor is one of the six sanctioned host-to-engine bridge
boundaries (rule **H4**). NumPy arrays cross the bridge exactly
once here; afterwards the data is owned by Lucid's engine. The
resulting layout is C-contiguous row-major:



$$
\text{stride}[i] = e \cdot \prod_{j=i+1}^{d-1} s_j
$$

where $e$ is the element size in bytes and $s_j$ are
the dimension sizes.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Fake-quantize the weight, run linear, fake-quantize the output.

methods.name: from_float
methods.summary: Build a QAT `Linear` from a float one (shares the trained weight).

members.extended:
A trainable float `lucid.nn.Linear` that fake-quantizes both the weight
and the output on every forward through a straight-through estimator (STE), letting
the network experience quantization rounding and learn to compensate while training
in full precision.  Inserted by `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`;
`lucid.quantization.convert` reads the trained weight and the observers' final
qparams to build an inference `lucid.nn.quantized.Linear`.

parameters.name: in_features
parameters.description: Size of each input sample.

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Size of each output sample.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Whether to add a learnable (float) bias term.

parameters.name: qconfig
parameters.description:
Quantization recipe supplying the weight and activation
`lucid.quantization.FakeQuantize` modules applied during training.

notes:
The STE rounds the weight in the forward pass but passes gradients straight through,
so the float weight stays fully trainable.

members.name: LinearReLU
members.signature: LinearReLU(in_features: int, out_features: int, bias: bool = True, qconfig: QConfig | None = None)

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Fake-quantize the weight, run linear, ReLU, fake-quantize the output.

methods.name: from_float
methods.summary: Build from a fused float `nni.LinearReLU` (its inner linear).

members.extended:
Behaves like `Linear`, but the activation fake-quant observes the range
*after* the fused ReLU, so the calibrated output grid reflects the true
(non-negative) inference range.  Built from a fused float
`lucid.nn.intrinsic.LinearReLU` by
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`; `lucid.quantization.convert` folds it
into a single quantized `lucid.nn.quantized.LinearReLU`.

parameters.name: in_features
parameters.description: Size of each input sample.

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Size of each output sample.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Whether to add a learnable (float) bias term.

parameters.name: qconfig
parameters.description:
Quantization recipe supplying the weight and activation
`lucid.quantization.FakeQuantize` modules applied during training.

notes:
The weight and the post-ReLU output are both fake-quantized every forward via a
straight-through estimator, keeping the float weight fully trainable.

members.name: Embedding
members.signature: Embedding(num_embeddings: int, embedding_dim: int, padding_idx: int | None = None, qconfig: QConfig | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_embeddings: int, embedding_dim: int, padding_idx: int | None = None, qconfig: QConfig | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise the MSELoss module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Fake-quantize the table, then look up `x`.

methods.name: from_float
methods.summary: Build a QAT `Embedding` from a float one (shares the trained table).

members.extended:
A trainable float `lucid.nn.Embedding` whose lookup table is fake-quantized
on every forward through a straight-through estimator (STE), so the learned
embeddings survive the eventual int8 storage.  Inserted by
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`; `lucid.quantization.convert` bakes the
trained, fake-quantized table into a quantized
`lucid.nn.quantized.Embedding`.

parameters.name: num_embeddings
parameters.description: Number of rows in the lookup table (vocabulary size).

parameters.name: embedding_dim
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each embedding vector.

parameters.name: padding_idx
parameters.description:
If given, the embedding at this index stays fixed and does not contribute to
the gradient.

parameters.name: qconfig
parameters.description:
Quantization recipe (keyword-only) supplying the weight
`lucid.quantization.FakeQuantize` applied to the table during training.

notes:
Only the table (weight) is fake-quantized — there is no activation observer, since
the output is a gather of already-quantized rows.

extended:
Float-trainable layers that fake-quantize their weights and outputs (via the
straight-through estimator) so a network learns to be robust to int8
inference.  Produced by `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat`; turned into
real quantized inference layers by `lucid.quantization.convert`.
